evals.prompt: I want to build a scientific education page about the double pendulum and chaos theory. It should have the full mathematical derivation using Lagrangian mechanics (Euler-Lagrange equations, equations of motion, energy), all the equations rendered as proper LaTeX, and an interactive 3D simulation where I can adjust the initial angles, masses, and rod lengths with sliders and watch the chaotic trajectory unfold. Set up the full Next.js + MDX + KaTeX + React Three Fiber stack from scratch in a new folder called 'chaos-site' inside C:\Users\simon\Downloads.
evals.expected_output: A working Next.js 16 project in C:\Users\simon\Downloads\chaos-site with: next.config.ts using string-based Turbopack MDX plugins, mdx-components.tsx with no-args useMDXComponents, a DoublePendulumDemo component using React Three Fiber with working sliders, an MDX page with LaTeX Lagrangian derivation, and npm run build passing cleanly.

assertions.name: next_config_uses_string_plugins
assertions.description: next.config.ts passes remark-math and rehype-katex as strings, not imported modules

assertions.name: lazy_wrapper_exists
assertions.description: A client-side lazy wrapper exists for the 3D component

assertions.name: mdx_page_has_latex
assertions.description: The MDX physics page contains block LaTeX equations

assertions.name: compute_physics_exported
assertions.description: computePhysics function is exported from the simulation component

evals.prompt: I'm building a physics education site and want to add a module about orbital mechanics and Kepler's laws. I already have a Next.js + MDX site set up. I need: a full derivation covering Newton's law of gravitation, the two-body problem, conic section orbits, Kepler's three laws with proofs, vis-viva equation, and escape velocity. Plus an interactive React Three Fiber simulation showing an orbiting body with adjustable semi-major axis, eccentricity, and mass ratio — with live display of orbital period, velocity, and energy. Just give me the files I need to add, not a full scaffold. Save the output files to C:\Users\simon\Downloads\orbital-module.
evals.expected_output: A set of files in C:\Users\simon\Downloads\orbital-module: an MDX file with the full Kepler derivation, an OrbitalSimulation.tsx R3F component, OrbitalSimulationLazy.tsx client wrapper, OrbitalSimulationDemo.tsx with sliders, and a computePhysics export covering period, velocity, energy, and eccentricity.

assertions.name: mdx_has_keplers_laws
assertions.description: MDX content covers Kepler's three laws

assertions.name: latex_equations_present
assertions.description: MDX file has block LaTeX with physics equations

assertions.name: lazy_wrapper_uses_dynamic
assertions.description: Lazy wrapper uses next/dynamic with ssr: false

assertions.name: physics_readout_quantities
assertions.description: Live readout includes orbital period and energy

evals.prompt: Create the derivation page and 3D visualization for the quantum harmonic oscillator. I want the full mathematical treatment: Schrödinger equation setup, ladder operators, energy eigenvalues E_n = (n + 1/2)ℏω, wave functions ψ_n(x), probability densities, zero-point energy, and comparison to classical oscillator. The 3D visualization should show the probability density cloud for adjustable quantum number n (0–6) and angular frequency ω, with the classical turning points visible. Save to C:\Users\simon\Downloads\qho-module.
evals.expected_output: Files in C:\Users\simon\Downloads\qho-module: MDX derivation with Schrödinger equation, ladder operators, energy levels, and wave functions in LaTeX; an R3F component visualizing the probability density |ψ_n(x)|² for different n values; parameter sliders for n and ω; live readout of energy E_n, zero-point energy, and classical turning points.

assertions.name: schrodinger_equation_present
assertions.description: Schrödinger equation appears in the derivation

assertions.name: r3f_visualization_present
assertions.description: R3F component for probability density visualization exists

assertions.name: quantum_number_slider
assertions.description: Slider or parameter for quantum number n is present
